methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

data_flows.trust_changes: User-supplied value directly cast to int128; overflow possible for extremely large values
manipulation_risks: int128 cast of _value could overflow silently for values > type(int128).max, though practically CANTO supply limits this
Lock end time rounding to weeks creates slight voting power variation

path: bias/slope written to gauge points_weight and points_sum
transformations: gauge weight aggregated into type sum and total
data_flows.trust_changes: Trust transitions from VotingEscrow (trusted source) to GaugeController calculations; no re-verification of lock validity beyond lock_end > next_time
exposure_risks: All vote data is public, enabling front-running of gauge weight votes
manipulation_risks: User can vote, then immediately delegate their veCANTO lock - the slope read from VE at vote time may not reflect future delegated state
Stale voting power: user's slope from getLastUserPoint may differ from actual current voting power if no checkpoint was done

transformations: block delta * cantoPerBlock * relative_weight / marketSupply → accCantoPerShare increment
data_flows.trust_changes: CRITICAL: msg.sender is trusted as lending market without verification. Any address that has a liquidityGauges mapping entry can indirectly call sync_ledger for a whitelisted market.
manipulation_risks: Any external contract can call sync_ledger directly - if there's no liquidity gauge, msg.sender must be the whitelisted market itself, but the liquidityGauges two-way mapping could create unintended aliases
Precision loss in accCantoPerShare due to uint128 truncation and integer division

path: sync_ledger called on LendingLedger for from and to addresses
update_market and user balance updates in LendingLedger
transformations: ERC20 amount → int256 delta (negative for from, positive for to)
data_flows.trust_changes: LiquidityGauge is trusted by LendingLedger via liquidityGauges mapping. Any ERC20 transfer triggers ledger sync.
exposure_risks: Transfer hook is called for every token movement including direct transfers between users
manipulation_risks: Double-counting: on transfer from A to B, sync_ledger is called twice (once negative for A, once positive for B). Both calls trigger update_market separately, which is fine but could have gas implications
Fee-on-transfer underlying tokens would cause minted gauge tokens to exceed actual underlying held

data_flows.source: Governance sets rewards via setRewards and funds contract
transformations: accumulated into accCantoPerShare per block delta
sinks: CANTO transfer to user on claim
data_flows.trust_changes: Governance is fully trusted for reward setting and funding
validation_points: Only epoch alignment checked for setRewards
No check that contract has sufficient CANTO balance
manipulation_risks: No validation that the contract holds enough CANTO to pay all accumulated rewards - could lead to failed claims
setRewards has no upper bound, governance could set impossibly high rewards

path: update_market uses these to compute epochTime
data_flows.trust_changes: Governance-set parameters directly affect reward calculations for all users
manipulation_risks: Malicious or incorrect parameters could cause epochTime to be wildly inaccurate, directing rewards incorrectly
No requirement to call update_market for all markets before changing parameters, despite comment saying so

transformations: newLocked.delegated set to 0 AFTER storing to locked[] but BEFORE checkpoint
data_flows.trust_changes: User retrieves their locked CANTO after lock expiry
validation_points: require lock not expired or unlockOverride
require lock not delegated to another user
exposure_risks: Reentrancy via CANTO transfer - mitigated by nonReentrant
manipulation_risks: The line `newLocked.delegated = 0` after `locked[msg.sender] = newLocked` means the checkpoint uses a different delegated value than what was stored. The stored value has delegated = locked_.delegated - amount, but checkpoint sees delegated = 0. This is intentional for zeroing out voting power but creates a discrepancy in the stored locked state vs checkpoint state.

core_rules: Users lock CANTO for fixed 5-year period, receiving linearly-decaying voting power
Every action (createLock, increaseAmount) resets the lock to 5 years from current time
Gauge weights determine proportion of CANTO rewards directed to each market
Users allocate voting power in basis points (0.01% granularity) across gauges
Rewards are calculated per-block within epochs of 100,000 blocks
Liquidity providers earn rewards proportional to their share of market supply
economic_incentives: Lock CANTO → get veCANTO voting power → vote on gauge weights → direct emissions
Provide liquidity → earn proportional CANTO rewards based on gauge weight
Longer remaining lock = more voting power (but lock is always 5 years)
No penalty for early withdrawal when unlockOverride is true
governance_mechanisms: Governance can add/remove gauges and change type weights
Governance sets per-block reward rates per epoch
Governance can toggle unlock override for emergency withdrawals
Governance can update block time parameters affecting reward calculations

edge_cases.scenario: VotingEscrow: createLock with value that overflows int128
edge_cases.actual_behavior: int128(int256(_value)) silently truncates if _value > type(int128).max (≈1.7e38). Practically impossible with CANTO supply but theoretically unsafe
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Would require sending more CANTO than exists

edge_cases.scenario: LendingLedger: update_market with epochTime calculation using stale or manipulated block time parameters
edge_cases.expected_behavior: epochTime should closely approximate actual wall-clock time
edge_cases.actual_behavior: epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000. The same epochTime is used for ALL iterations of the while loop regardless of the actual epoch being processed - it always uses current block.number
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: The epochTime should vary as `i` progresses through historical blocks, but it's fixed to the current block's estimated time. This means gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the same time for all historical epochs, potentially applying current-period weights to past epochs.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Accumulated rewards per share should be precise
edge_cases.actual_behavior: uint128 max is ~3.4e38. With 1e18 scaling, this limits effective reward tracking. If cantoPerBlock is very high and marketSupply is very low (e.g., 1 wei), accCantoPerShare could overflow uint128
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A user who is the sole depositor with minimal amount could cause accCantoPerShare to overflow, wrapping around and losing rewards for all users

edge_cases.scenario: GaugeController: vote_for_gauge_weights on removed gauge with weight > 0
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should not be possible to add weight to removed gauge
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The check is `require(_user_weight == 0 || gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] != 0)` which correctly allows only 0 weight votes on removed gauges. But the subsequent `gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1` would underflow to -1 (int128) for removed gauges when _user_weight is 0
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: When voting 0 on a removed gauge (gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] == 0), the code proceeds past the require but then computes gauge_type = 0 - 1 = -1. This accesses points_sum[-1] and changes_sum[-1], corrupting unrelated storage slots. The `require(gauge_type >= 0)` check only verifies the gauge is added, which is contradicted by the earlier check allowing 0-weight votes on non-gauges.

edge_cases.scenario: LendingLedger: sync_ledger called by arbitrary address with liquidityGauge mapping
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only legitimate lending markets or their gauges should sync
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The liquidityGauges mapping is bidirectional: liquidityGauges[market] = gauge AND liquidityGauges[gauge] = market. If msg.sender is a gauge address, it maps to the market. If msg.sender is the market directly, it maps to the gauge. But any address not in the mapping passes through as-is and must be whitelisted.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: The design relies on only the LiquidityGauge contract calling sync_ledger with itself as msg.sender. But anyone could deploy a contract that calls sync_ledger directly. If that contract's address happens to not be in liquidityGauges, the address itself is used as lendingMarket and must be whitelisted - so this is actually safe for non-mapped addresses. The gauge address IS in the mapping, so only the gauge can route to its market.

edge_cases.scenario: VotingEscrow: withdraw sets newLocked.delegated differently for checkpoint vs storage
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Consistent state between storage and checkpoint
edge_cases.actual_behavior: locked[msg.sender] = newLocked (with delegated = locked_.delegated - amount), then newLocked.delegated = 0, then _checkpoint(msg.sender, locked_, newLocked) uses delegated=0. Storage has non-zero delegated if there was extra delegation.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If a user has self-delegation only (delegatee == msg.sender required), delegated should equal amount. After subtracting amount, delegated becomes 0. So stored.delegated = 0 and checkpoint sees 0. Seems consistent for the self-delegation case. But if somehow delegated != amount (rounding or bugs), there could be a discrepancy.

edge_cases.scenario: GaugeController: _get_weight and _get_sum underflow on slope subtraction
edge_cases.actual_behavior: In _get_weight: pt.slope -= d_slope without underflow check. If d_slope > pt.slope (e.g., due to gauge removal modifying changes_weight), this will revert on Solidity 0.8+ checked arithmetic, potentially bricking the gauge
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: After remove_gauge_weight zeroes out changes_weight for 263 weeks, any slope changes beyond that window remain. Also, votes placed after gauge removal could create inconsistent slope change data. If _get_weight reverts, it blocks all operations that call it including vote_for_gauge_weights for any gauge of the same type.

edge_cases.scenario: LendingLedger: whiteListLendingMarket sets lastRewardBlock only when whitelisting
edge_cases.expected_behavior: When re-whitelisting a market, lastRewardBlock should be updated
edge_cases.actual_behavior: lastRewardBlock is set to current block.number when whitelisting. If a market is delisted then re-listed, the old lastRewardBlock from the first listing might still be present if the market was already initialized (the code uses marketInfo[_market] which could be the gauge address, not _market, creating confusion)
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: When whitelisting with _hasGauge=true, the market address in the whitelist is _market but marketInfo is also keyed by _market. However, sync_ledger resolves to the gauge address via liquidityGauges mapping. So marketInfo[gauge_address] is used in update_market, but whiteListLendingMarket sets marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock. If _market != gauge_address, the lastRewardBlock is set on the wrong key.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Whitelist check in update_market should match the resolved market address
edge_cases.actual_behavior: whiteListLendingMarket sets lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] = true and marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock. But in sync_ledger, if liquidityGauges[msg.sender] != address(0), lendingMarket becomes the gauge address. update_market then checks lendingMarketWhitelist[gauge_address] which was never set to true. The whitelist is set for _market (the underlying market), not the gauge.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: This means markets with liquidity gauges cannot function: sync_ledger resolves to the gauge address, but update_market requires lendingMarketWhitelist[gauge_address] to be true, which it never is. The system is fundamentally broken for gauge-enabled markets unless the governance separately whitelists the gauge address too.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Minted gauge tokens should match received underlying
edge_cases.actual_behavior: depositUnderlying mints _amount tokens regardless of actual amount received via safeTransferFrom
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If underlying token has transfer fees, users receive more gauge tokens than underlying held, allowing last withdrawer to fail or draining other users' tokens

edge_cases.scenario: VotingEscrow: increaseAmount resets lock time but doesn't update delegatee's lock end
edge_cases.expected_behavior: When a delegated lock's amount is increased, the delegatee's voting power should reflect the new lock end
edge_cases.actual_behavior: increaseAmount sets newLocked.end = _floorToWeek(block.timestamp + LOCKTIME) for the sender, but the delegatee's lock end is not updated. The delegatee's checkpoint uses the delegatee's own lock end time for slope/bias calculations. The delegated amount increases on the delegatee, but their lock end might expire sooner.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If delegatee's lock expires before the delegator's new lock end, the delegated voting power is calculated based on delegatee's shorter lock, effectively losing voting power that should exist.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: All future slope changes for the gauge should be cancelled
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Loop runs for 263 iterations (263 * WEEK ≈ 5.05 years). Since LOCKTIME is 1825 days ≈ 260.7 weeks, 263 weeks should cover all possible lock expirations. However, if any lock ends beyond 263 weeks from next_time, those slope changes are missed.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Edge case if locks can extend slightly beyond 263 weeks due to rounding

assumption_violations: LendingLedger assumes liquidityGauges mapping correctly resolves msg.sender to whitelisted market, but the whitelist is set on the original market address while sync_ledger resolves to the gauge address
LendingLedger assumes block times are stable and governance will update parameters before significant drift, but no enforcement mechanism exists

GaugeController assumes gauge_types_[addr] is always > 0 for valid gauges, but vote_for_gauge_weights allows 0-weight votes on removed gauges where gauge_types_ == 0, leading to underflow in gauge_type calculation
LiquidityGauge assumes underlying token transfers exact amounts (no fee-on-transfer)
VotingEscrow assumes CANTO transfers always succeed or revert cleanly via low-level call
LendingLedger update_market uses the same epochTime for all historical block iterations, assuming time doesn't change significantly within the catch-up loop
game_theory_risks: Users can vote on gauge weights and then delegate their veCANTO, effectively double-counting influence if the system doesn't invalidate gauge votes on delegation
Large CANTO holders can concentrate voting power on a single gauge to capture most emissions for a market they dominate
Last-mover advantage in gauge voting: users can observe others' votes and optimally allocate remaining weight
Flash-loan-like attacks are somewhat mitigated by the 5-year lock requirement, but getLastUserPoint reads could be stale
Users could rapidly increase amount to get more gauge votes, as increaseAmount doesn't require a cooldown on voting
integration_risks: GaugeController reads VotingEscrow state via getLastUserPoint and lockEnd, which could be stale if no checkpoint was recently made
LendingLedger calls gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write which triggers state changes in GaugeController, creating cross-contract state dependencies
LiquidityGauge _afterTokenTransfer calls back to LendingLedger.sync_ledger on every transfer, creating a tight coupling that could cause unexpected reverts
No interface enforcement between LendingLedger and lending markets - any contract can call sync_ledger
calculation_errors: LendingLedger update_market: epochTime is computed once using current block.number but used for all historical epoch iterations. It should be computed per-iteration based on the block number being processed, not the current block

LendingLedger: accCantoPerShare accumulation uses (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * gauge_relative_weight) / marketSupply without scaling by 1e18 first, potentially losing precision when cantoPerBlock * weight product is small relative to marketSupply

GaugeController vote_for_gauge_weights: when voting 0 weight on a removed gauge (gauge_types_ == 0), gauge_type = 0 - 1 = -1 (int128), which is negative. The subsequent require(gauge_type >= 0) would catch this and revert, meaning users CANNOT remove their votes from removed gauges, locking their vote_user_power permanently

VotingEscrow: in withdraw(), delegated is decreased by amountToSend (the full amount), then set to 0 before checkpoint. If user had delegated to themselves AND received delegation from others, the delegated field could be > amount, and the subtraction followed by zeroing would be inconsistent. However, the require(delegatee == msg.sender) check and the fact that only self-delegated users can withdraw provides some protection.

token_semantics.notes: Native chain token used for VotingEscrow locks and LendingLedger rewards. Transferred via low-level call with value.
Non-transferable voting power token. balanceOf decays linearly over time. Not an actual ERC20 - only has balanceOf/totalSupply view functions.

1:1 wrapper of underlying cNOTE-like tokens. _afterTokenTransfer hooks call LendingLedger.sync_ledger. Inherits ERC20Burnable allowing arbitrary burns. Assumes underlying has standard transfer semantics.
Assumed standard ERC20 lending market token. If fee-on-transfer, gauge accounting breaks.

invariants.name: voting_power_conservation
invariants.notes: Global bias should equal sum of all user biases at any checkpoint, modulo rounding from integer division

invariants.name: vote_power_allocation
invariants.notes: Total allocated power per user should not exceed 10000 bps. BUG: power can get permanently locked if gauge is removed and user cannot vote 0 due to gauge_type underflow

invariants.name: lending_market_balance
invariants.notes: Total market balance should equal sum of all user amounts

invariants.name: gauge_token_backing
invariants.notes: Gauge total supply should equal underlying balance held. Violated if fee-on-transfer tokens used.

invariants.name: reward_solvency
state_vars: sum(pending_rewards for all users across all markets)
invariants.notes: Contract CANTO balance should cover all pending rewards. No enforcement mechanism exists.
